title: Cirsium helenioides
taxonomy: Cirsium helenioides is a species of thistle that belongs to the family Asteraceae. It is a member of the genus Cirsium, which consists of various species of thistles. The genus Cirsium is further classified under the subfamily Carduoideae and the order Asterales. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics and its evolutionary relationships with other plants. The classification of Cirsium helenioides is used by botanists to understand the plant's identity and its position within the plant kingdom.
uses: Cirsium helenioides, also known as Helen's thistle or Rocky Mountain thistle, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant is said to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which may help in treating wounds, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. In some herbal remedies, Cirsium helenioides is used to relieve symptoms of colds, flu, and other respiratory infections. The plant is also believed to have antioxidant and antibacterial properties, which may contribute to its potential health benefits. Further research is needed to confirm the effectiveness and safety of using Cirsium helenioides for medicinal purposes.

constituents: Cirsium helenioides, also known as Helen's purple thistle, is a plant species in the family Asteraceae. Cirsium helenioides contains sesquiterpene lactones, specifically helenin and helenalin, which are known for their anti-inflammatory properties. intro: Cirsium helenioides, also known as Helen's plume thistle, is a perennial plant species native to North America, characterized by its distinctive purple flowers and feathery foliage. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in the treatment of various ailments such as wounds, digestive issues, and respiratory problems. Bioactive constituents of Cirsium helenioides include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties.
